question: What are coroutines and how do they differ from threads?
answer:
Coroutines are lightweight concurrency primitives that suspend without blocking the underlying thread. A `suspend` function can pause and resume; the underlying thread is free to run other coroutines while it waits.

Vs threads:
• Tens of thousands of coroutines easily co-exist on a small thread pool — threads cost megabytes of stack each.
• Suspension is cooperative; a coroutine only suspends at suspension points (`suspend fun`, `delay`, channel send/receive).
• Cancellation is cooperative (CancellationException propagates).
• Structured concurrency — coroutines are launched into a CoroutineScope, parent waits for children, cancellation cascades.
• Coroutines run on a CoroutineDispatcher (Default, IO, Main, Unconfined, custom).

Think of coroutines as Kotlin's answer to async/await + structured concurrency.

question: What are CoroutineScope, Job, and structured concurrency?
answer:
Structured concurrency in coroutines is enforced by three concepts:

• CoroutineScope — defines lifetime + context (Job + Dispatcher). All coroutines launched inside it are children of its Job.
• Job — represents the lifecycle of a coroutine (Active, Cancelling, Cancelled, Completed). Jobs have parent/child relationships.
• Cancellation propagates — cancelling a parent cancels all children; a failing child cancels parent (unless SupervisorJob).

In practice you never call `GlobalScope.launch` — it has no parent and leaks tasks. Use `viewModelScope`, `lifecycleScope`, `coroutineScope { }` (suspending, awaits children), or your own scope tied to a class lifecycle.

question: What are Dispatchers (Default, IO, Main, Unconfined)?
answer:
A CoroutineDispatcher decides which thread (or thread pool) a coroutine runs on.

• `Dispatchers.Default` — for CPU-bound work (parsing, sorting). Backed by a thread pool sized to CPU count.
• `Dispatchers.IO` — for blocking I/O (disk, network when not non-blocking). Thread pool that elastically scales (up to 64 by default).
• `Dispatchers.Main` — Android / Swing main thread. Use for UI updates.
• `Dispatchers.Main.immediate` — Main but skips re-dispatch if already on Main; lower latency for UI updates.
• `Dispatchers.Unconfined` — runs in caller thread until first suspension; mostly for tests / advanced cases.
• Custom dispatchers — `Executor.asCoroutineDispatcher()` for fine-grained control.

Switch dispatchers with `withContext(Dispatchers.X) { ... }`.

question: What is Flow? How is it different from suspending functions or Channel?
answer:
A `Flow<T>` is a cold asynchronous stream. Like a `Sequence` but suspending — values are produced asynchronously and consumed via `collect`.

• Cold — nothing happens until `collect` is called; each collector triggers a fresh execution.
• Backpressure-aware — emit suspends until the collector is ready.
• Cancellation — cancelling the collector stops production.
• Operators — map, filter, debounce, combine, flatMapMerge/flatMapConcat/flatMapLatest, retry.

Vs alternatives:
• `suspend fun` returns ONE value; `Flow` returns many over time.
• `Channel` is hot — values can be lost if no receiver. Use channels for pipelines/queues; flows for streams.

question: What are StateFlow and SharedFlow? When to use which?
answer:
StateFlow and SharedFlow are HOT Flow implementations.

• StateFlow<T> — like a behavior subject in Rx. Holds a single current value, replays it to every new collector. `.value` is read/write. Conflated — only the latest value matters; intermediate updates may be skipped if collectors are slow.
• SharedFlow<T> — flexible event/state hub. Configurable replay count, buffer, overflow (DROP_OLDEST, SUSPEND, DROP_LATEST). No initial value required.

Idiomatic use:
• StateFlow — UI state (loading, list, current user) where new collectors must see the current snapshot. Replaces LiveData in modern Android.
• SharedFlow — one-shot UI events (snackbar, navigation), domain events. Use replay = 0 + buffer.

question: How do you handle exceptions and cancellation in coroutines?
answer:
Two orthogonal concerns:

Cancellation:
• Cooperative — coroutines must regularly check `isActive` or call suspending functions. Tight CPU loops without suspension don't cancel.
• `CancellationException` is propagated, not handled — DO NOT catch it generically. Use `currentCoroutineContext().ensureActive()` to surface it inside suspending logic.
• `withContext(NonCancellable) { ... }` — block to run cleanup that must complete even if parent is cancelled.

Exceptions:
• Default Job — first child failure cancels the parent and all siblings.
• SupervisorJob / supervisorScope — failures stay isolated.
• `CoroutineExceptionHandler` — catch-all for uncaught exceptions in launches; only invoked at root scopes.
• `async` returns a Deferred — exceptions surface on `await()`, not at launch.
